gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Previous extracellular L-lactate biosensor eLACCO1.1 had low ΔF/F (only 43% anionic chromophore in lactate-bound state), slow kinetics, and suboptimal membrane localization. Intracellular red biosensors were lacking or had poor performance. The goal was to develop improved green extracellular and red intracellular biosensors with higher ΔF/F, faster kinetics, and better localization for multiplexed imaging.

section_gists.heading: Development of a second-generation green fluorescent L-lactate biosensor, eLACCO2.1
section_gists.gist: Starting from eLACCO0.9 (faster than eLACCO1), directed evolution over 8 rounds (2 rounds random mutagenesis → eLACCO1.2, Cys340Ser → eLACCO1.3, 6 more rounds → eLACCO2) improved ΔF/F from 3.9 to 16. Affinity tuning via Leu79Ile produced eLACCO2.1 with Kd 1.9 mM and ΔF/F 14 (3.5× eLACCO1.1). Non-responsive control deLACCO1 created by Asp444Asn + 3 rounds brightness evolution.
process_signals: eLACCO0.9 chosen for faster kinetics over eLACCO1.
Cys340Ser introduced to avoid disulfide bond complications.
Affinity tuned because eLACCO2 Kd (280 µM) too low for extracellular mM range.
Leu79Ile selected from 10 variants for best balance of affinity and ΔF/F.
methods: Directed evolution by error-prone PCR of whole gene.
Screening of crude protein extracts for ΔF/F in 96-well plates.
Site-directed mutagenesis for affinity tuning and control biosensor.
failures_or_limits: eLACCO2 affinity too high for extracellular use.
Some variants in affinity tuning likely had lower ΔF/F.

section_gists.heading: Development of a red fluorescent biosensor for intracellular L-lactate, R-iLACCO1
section_gists.gist: R-iLACCO1 built by inserting cpmApple (from R-eLACCO1) into LldR at position 109. Initial R-iLACCO0.1 dim with ΔF/F 0.7. Linker optimization (2 aa deletion in C-terminal linker) improved brightness (R-iLACCO0.2, ΔF/F 0.6). 11 rounds of evolution (1 linker + 10 random mutagenesis) yielded R-iLACCO1 with ΔF/F 20. Lower affinity variants R-iLACCO1.1 and 1.2 identified by screening at multiple lactate concentrations. Control R-diLACCO1 (Asp69Asn + 2 rounds brightness).
process_signals: cpmApple chosen over TTHA0766-based RFP to avoid Ca2+ dependence.
Insertion site Leu109/Val110 in LldR.
Linker lengths critical for brightness.
Screening at 0, 0.5, 10 mM lactate to find lower affinity variants.
methods: Directed evolution: site-directed random mutagenesis or error-prone PCR.
Screening in E. coli crude extracts.
failures_or_limits: Initial linker variants may have had poor performance.

section_gists.heading: Optimization of cell surface localization
section_gists.gist: Screened 23 leaders and 10 anchors with eLACCO1.1. NGR GPI anchor gave best localization and higher ΔF/F vs CD59. HA leader with NGR anchor gave best signal in neurites. Final construct: HA-eLACCO2.1-NGR.
process_signals: Previous work showed leader/anchor combination critical.
Screening done in primary neurons.
CD59-eLACCO1.1-CD59 had puncta and lower ΔF/F.
methods: Fluorescence imaging to assess localization efficiency.
ΔF/F measurement in presence of lactate.
failures_or_limits: Many leader/anchor combinations likely gave poor localization or low response.

section_gists.heading: Characterization of eLACCO2.1 in live mammalian cells and acute brain slices
section_gists.gist: eLACCO2.1 showed good membrane localization, ΔF/F 8.8 in HeLa (138% of eLACCO1.1), faster kinetics (τon 57 s, τoff 20 s), photostability comparable to EGFP. In neurons, ΔF/F 8.1 (5.4× eLACCO1.1). In astrocytes, ΔF/F 7.3. In brain slices, detected endogenous lactate changes: decrease upon aglycemia, increase upon theta burst stimulation.
process_signals: eLACCO2.1 outperforms eLACCO1.1 in all metrics.
Ca2+ dependence noted but Kd (270 µM) below physiological extracellular Ca2+.
deLACCO1 control shows no response.
methods: HeLa cell imaging with lactate perfusion.
AAV transduction in mouse cortex, acute slice imaging, two-photon microscopy.
failures_or_limits: Ca2+ sensitivity could cause artifacts if extracellular Ca2+ drops below ~1 mM.

section_gists.heading: Characterization of R-iLACCO1 in live mammalian cells
section_gists.gist: R-iLACCO1 variants compared with existing sensors. R-iLACCO1.2 had highest ΔF/F in HeLa (11.2) and neurons (0.31). All R-iLACCO variants showed uniform localization, unlike some competitors. Table 3 summarizes performance.
process_signals: R-iLACCO1.2 chosen for multiplexing due to lower affinity and high ΔF/F.
Competitors (CanlonicSF, Green Lindoblum, GEM-IL, FiLa, Laconic) had lower ΔF/F or punctate localization.
methods: Expression in HeLa, HEK293T, neurons, astrocytes.
ΔF/F measurement with lactate or MCT inhibitor treatment.
failures_or_limits: R-iLACCO1.1 and 1.2 have lower ΔF/F than R-iLACCO1 in purified form but better in cells due to affinity matching.

section_gists.heading: In vivo imaging of eLACCO2.1 and R-iLACCO1.1 in mice
section_gists.gist: eLACCO2.1 responded to i.c.v. lactate injection in awake mice; deLACCO1 did not. eLACCO2.1 detected endogenous lactate decrease upon insulin i.p. in freely moving mice. R-iLACCO1.1 detected lactate increase in somatosensory cortex upon whisker stimulation.
process_signals: eLACCO2.1 functional in vivo.
R-iLACCO1.1 used for intracellular in vivo imaging.
methods: AAV expression in visual or somatosensory cortex.
Fiber photometry in freely moving mice.
failures_or_limits: No major failures reported, but in vivo signals may be small.

section_gists.heading: Spectrally and spatially multiplexed L-lactate imaging in glioblastoma cells
section_gists.gist: Coexpressed eLACCO2.1 and R-iLACCO1.2 in glioblastoma cells. Glucose stimulation increased both signals; intracellular signal showed transient peak coinciding with slower extracellular rise, suggesting MCT-mediated export. Also targeted R-iLACCO1.2 to mitochondria and ER, showing lactate shuttling to these organelles.
process_signals: Multiplexing enabled by spectral separation (green/red).
Transient peak in cytosol not seen with MCT inhibitor.
Organelle targeting demonstrates versatility.
failures_or_limits: No major failures, but interpretation of transient peak relies on inhibitor experiment.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: eLACCO2.1 improved by increasing anionic chromophore fraction (76% vs 43% in eLACCO1.1). Leader/anchor optimization critical. eLACCO2.1 outperforms eLACCO1.1 in localization, ΔF/F, kinetics. Ca2+ sensitivity may cause artifacts if extracellular Ca2+ drops to ~1 mM. Red biosensors enable multiplexing; R-iLACCO1 series outperforms existing sensors. Future work: further improve red sensor brightness and develop sensors for other metabolites.
process_signals: Rationale for directed evolution: increase anionic chromophore.
Acknowledgment of Ca2+ limitation.
Comparison table shows R-iLACCO1.2 best overall.
failures_or_limits: Ca2+ interference possible during extreme neural activity.
Red sensor brightness still lower than green.
Photostability of eLACCO2.1 lower in lactate-bound state.

section_gists.heading: General methods and materials

section_gists.heading: Engineering of eLACCO2.1 and R-iLACCO1
section_gists.gist: Detailed directed evolution protocols: eLACCO2.1 from eLACCO0.9 via 8 rounds (2→eLACCO1.2, C340S→eLACCO1.3, 6→eLACCO2, L79I→eLACCO2.1). R-iLACCO1: cpmApple inserted into LldR, linker optimization, 11 rounds (1 linker + 10 random) → R-iLACCO1, with affinity variants from final round screening at multiple lactate concentrations. Control biosensors made by binding site mutation + brightness evolution.
process_signals: Library size: 200-400 colonies per round.
Screening in 96-well plates with plate reader.
Error-prone PCR for random mutagenesis.
Site-directed mutagenesis for specific changes.
failures_or_limits: No explicit failures, but many rounds needed.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Improving the fraction of anionic chromophore in the lactate-bound state is key to increasing ΔF/F for green sensors.
Using LldR instead of TTHA0766 avoids Ca2+ dependence for intracellular red sensors.
Optimizing leader and anchor sequences is essential for efficient membrane targeting and sensor performance in neurons.
Lower affinity variants are needed to match physiological lactate ranges and avoid saturation.
Spectral orthogonality (green/red) enables simultaneous extracellular and intracellular lactate imaging.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the specific mutations in eLACCO2 and R-iLACCO1, and how did they affect chromophore environment?
Were there any intermediate variants with poor performance that guided evolution?
How was the screening assay calibrated to avoid false positives?
What is the exact mechanism of Ca2+ sensitivity in eLACCO2.1?
Can the red sensor brightness be further improved for deeper tissue imaging?

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed one round of C-terminal linker optimization, followed by ten rounds of directed evolution by random mutagenesis of the whole gene

R-iLACCO0.2, was subjected to an iterative process of library generation and screening in E. coli. Libraries were generated by site-directed random mutagenesis or error-prone PCR of the whole gene. For each round, approximately 200–400 fluorescent colonies were picked, cultured, and tested on 96-well plates under a plate reader. There were 11 rounds of screening before R-iLACCO1, R-iLACCO1.1, and R-iLACCO1.2 were identified.

Ultimately, we developed the R-iLACCO1 variant with high molecular brightness (11.3 in L-lactate-bound state, Table 2) and $\Delta F/F$ of 20 that is one of the highest fluorescence responses for red fluorescent biosensor protein, as measured using purified protein (Supplementary Table 2) $^{18,31}$ .

findings.problem: Step 2 incorrectly claims that Cys340Ser was introduced during the evolution of eLACCO0.9 to eLACCO2. In reality, Cys340Ser was introduced into eLACCO1 (a different starting point) to produce eLACCO1.3, not during the eLACCO0.9 lineage. The evidence (EV_AUTO_005, EV_AUTO_001) refers to eLACCO1, not eLACCO0.9. This misrepresents the evolutionary path.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove the mention of Cys340Ser from step 2. The directed evolution of eLACCO0.9 to eLACCO2 involved 8 rounds of random mutagenesis without the Cys340Ser mutation. Alternatively, clarify that Cys340Ser was part of the eLACCO1 lineage, not eLACCO0.9.

findings.problem: Step 3 states that eLACCO2.1 has Kd 1.9 mM and ΔF/F 14, but the evidence provided (EV_AUTO_022) only mentions ΔF/F 14 for eLACCO2.1 as a purified protein. The Kd value is not directly quoted in the step's evidence. While the Kd is likely correct, the step should include evidence that explicitly states the Kd.
findings.revision_instruction: Add evidence that explicitly states the Kd of eLACCO2.1 is 1.9 mM, or remove the Kd claim from the step if not directly supported.

findings.problem: Step 4 claims eLACCO2.1 in HeLa cells has ΔF/F 8.8, τon 57 s, τoff 20 s, and photostability comparable to EGFP. The only evidence provided (EV_AUTO_022) refers to purified protein ΔF/F of 14, not HeLa cell data. The specific numbers for HeLa are not supported by the cited evidence.
findings.revision_instruction: Provide evidence that directly supports the HeLa cell characterization numbers (ΔF/F 8.8, τon 57 s, τoff 20 s, photostability). If such evidence is not available, remove these specific numbers from the step.

findings.problem: Step 6 claims R-iLACCO0.1 had ΔF/F 0.7 and was dim, but the evidence (EV_C002_E06_02) only mentions linker optimization and directed evolution, not the initial ΔF/F. The ΔF/F 0.7 is not directly quoted.
findings.revision_instruction: Add evidence that explicitly states the initial ΔF/F of R-iLACCO0.1 is 0.7, or remove the number if not supported.

findings.problem: Step 7 claims R-iLACCO0.2 had ΔF/F 0.6, but the evidence (EV_C008_E02_01) does not mention this number. The ΔF/F 0.6 is not directly supported.
findings.revision_instruction: Add evidence that explicitly states the ΔF/F of R-iLACCO0.2 is 0.6, or remove the number if not supported.

findings.problem: Step 10 claims R-iLACCO1.2 shows ΔF/F 11.2 in HeLa, but the evidence (EV_C005_E04_01, EV_C006_E04_01, etc.) does not explicitly state this number. The ΔF/F 11.2 is not directly quoted.
findings.revision_instruction: Add evidence that explicitly states the ΔF/F of R-iLACCO1.2 in HeLa is 11.2, or remove the number if not supported.

findings.problem: Step 9 includes evidence EV_C009_E07_02 and EV_C009_E01_02, which are about theta burst stimulation in brain slices, unrelated to the affinity screening of R-iLACCO variants. This appears to be an erroneous inclusion.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove the irrelevant evidence EV_C009_E07_02 and EV_C009_E01_02 from step 9.

findings.problem: Step 1 claims to cover K04 (choosing eLACCO0.9 as starting point), but the step's decision and evidence do not explicitly mention eLACCO0.9 or the reason for choosing it. The step only covers the problem and design of the orthogonal pair.
findings.revision_instruction: Either add explicit mention of eLACCO0.9 selection and its rationale in step 1, or create a separate step for K04.

findings.problem: Step 3 claims to cover K13 (screening leaders and anchors), but the step's decision and evidence do not mention the screening of 23 leaders and 10 anchors or the selection of HA leader and NGR anchor. The step only covers affinity tuning.
findings.revision_instruction: Either add explicit mention of the leader/anchor screening in step 3, or create a separate step for K13.

must_keep: The overall trajectory of developing eLACCO2.1 and R-iLACCO1 series is correct.
The key evolutionary steps (directed evolution rounds, affinity tuning, linker optimization) are present.
The validation steps in brain slices, primary cells, and in vivo are included.
self_critic.summary: The candidate trajectory covers all required mainline events but has several issues with evidence support and factual accuracy. Step 2 incorrectly includes the Cys340Ser mutation in the eLACCO0.9 lineage. Multiple steps lack direct evidence for specific numbers (ΔF/F, Kd, kinetics). Some steps include irrelevant evidence or fail to explicitly cover assigned canonical events. Revision is needed to correct the evolutionary path and ensure all claims are properly evidenced.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed one round of C-terminal linker optimization, followed by ten rounds of directed evolution by random mutagenesis of the whole gene

R-iLACCO0.2, was subjected to an iterative process of library generation and screening in E. coli. Libraries were generated by site-directed random mutagenesis or error-prone PCR of the whole gene. For each round, approximately 200–400 fluorescent colonies were picked, cultured, and tested on 96-well plates under a plate reader. There were 11 rounds of screening before R-iLACCO1, R-iLACCO1.1, and R-iLACCO1.2 were identified.

Ultimately, we developed the R-iLACCO1 variant with high molecular brightness (11.3 in L-lactate-bound state, Table 2) and $\Delta F/F$ of 20 that is one of the highest fluorescence responses for red fluorescent biosensor protein, as measured using purified protein (Supplementary Table 2) $^{18,31}$ .

initial_request.research_problem: Develop improved green extracellular and red intracellular L-lactate biosensors with higher ΔF/F, faster kinetics, and better localization for multiplexed imaging.

constraints: Must enable spectrally and spatially multiplexed imaging
